KOPYTKA Z CEBULĄ

Kopytka, whose name means "little hooves" in Polish, are small potato dumplings shaped like tiny crescents that originated in the Silesian
highlands of southern Poland. These delicate pillows of potato and flour are lighter than their cousin pierogi and cook remarkably quickly.
Traditionally paired with caramelized onions (cebula), they represent peasant cooking at its finest—humble ingredients transformed through technique
into comfort food of the highest order. The sweet, deeply browned onions provide a rich contrast to the delicate, starchy dumplings. This dish
appears on every Polish family table, often as the main course rather than a side. Each family guards their own recipe variation jealously.

INGREDIENTS:

For Kopytka:
600g potatoes (floury varieties like Maris Piper)
200g all-purpose flour
1 egg
½ tsp salt
Pinch of white pepper
Nutmeg, small pinch

For Caramelized Onions:
600g yellow onions, sliced thinly
80g butter
1 tsp salt
½ tsp sugar
Freshly ground black pepper

METHOD - KOPYTKA:

1. Peel potatoes and cut into large chunks. Boil in salted water for 15-20 minutes until completely tender.

2. Drain thoroughly. Return to pot and mash until completely smooth—no lumps should remain.

3. Add egg, salt, pepper, and nutmeg. Mix well. Gradually add flour, mixing gently. Do not overwork—dough should be soft but manageable.

4. Bring large pot of salted water to rolling boil.

5. Shape dumplings: take small handful of dough and form into small crescents about 5cm long, pinching slightly to create the characteristic "hoove" shape.
Work quickly so dough doesn't dry out.

6. Drop kopytka into boiling water. They will initially sink, then rise to surface when cooked (about 2-3 minutes after rising).
Remove with slotted spoon.

METHOD - CARAMELIZED ONIONS:

1. While kopytka cook, begin caramelizing onions. Melt butter in large skillet over medium heat.

2. Add sliced onions and salt. Stir frequently, cooking for 30-40 minutes until onions turn deep golden brown.
This requires patience—rushing will result in merely softened onions rather than properly caramelized ones.

3. In final 5 minutes, add sugar and black pepper. Taste and adjust seasoning.

ASSEMBLY:

Divide warm kopytka into serving bowls. Top generously with hot caramelized onions and any butter from the pan.

SERVING: Serve immediately while hot. Some prefer a dollop of sour cream alongside.

NOTES: Kopytka dough is quite forgiving. If dough is too sticky, work in more flour one tablespoon at a time.
Do not refrigerate before cooking—they should be prepared and cooked immediately. The texture is best when freshly made.
